title: Eton Mess
subtitle: Crushed meringue, whipped cream, and fresh strawberries
excerpt: A deliberately casual British dessert of crumbled meringue, lightly whipped cream, and macerated strawberries folded together just before serving. The meringue can be made ahead and stored for days. Everything comes together in five minutes at the table.
sourceNotes: Eton Mess is associated with Eton College, where it has been served since at least the early twentieth century, traditionally at the annual cricket match against Harrow. The name suggests an accidental origin, a meringue and strawberry dessert dropped or crushed, though the exact story is contested. The dessert became widely popular in British cooking from the 1980s onward.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The meringues can be made up to five days ahead and stored in an airtight container. Macerate the strawberries up to 2 hours ahead. Assemble just before serving.

content.text: The meringue for Eton Mess should not be baked until completely dry and brittle, you want some chewiness at the centre. Bake at a low temperature until the outside is crisp and the meringue lifts cleanly from the paper, but press the underside with your thumb: it should still give slightly. If it is completely dry through, it will crumble to powder rather than break into pieces with texture.

Buy good meringue nests if you don't want to make your own, any supermarket meringue will work. The key to the assembled dish is the layering of textures: crisp meringue, soft cream, and the juice that runs from the macerated strawberries. Fold everything together very loosely so there are pockets of each element rather than a homogeneous pink mixture.

Heat the oven to 100°C fan. Line a large baking tray with baking paper. Whisk 3 egg whites with a pinch of cream of tartar (if using) in a clean, dry bowl until they form firm peaks. Add {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar one tablespoon at a time, whisking between additions, until the meringue is thick, glossy, and holds stiff peaks. Spoon in large irregular mounds onto the lined tray. Bake for 1 hour 30 minutes until the outside is crisp and the meringue lifts cleanly from the paper. Turn off the oven and leave inside until completely cool.

Toss {{strawberries}} of hulled and halved strawberries with 30 g of caster sugar and leave for at least 30 minutes at room temperature. The sugar will draw out the juice and create a light syrup around the fruit.

Whip {{double-cream}} of double cream to soft peaks, it should hold its shape but not be stiff. Break the cooled meringues into large rough pieces. Fold the cream, meringue, and strawberries together loosely, keeping visible pockets of each element. Spoon into glasses or bowls and serve immediately.

items.cause: Sugar wasn't fully dissolved during whisking, or the meringue was baked in a damp environment
items.fix: Whisk the meringue until the sugar is completely dissolved, rub a little between your fingers; if grainy, whisk more. Bake in a dry oven and cool completely before storing.

items.symptom: Assembled mess is a wet, uniform pink
items.cause: Strawberries released too much juice, or cream was over-whipped and collapsed under the weight
items.fix: Fold very loosely at the last moment. If the strawberries are very juicy, strain off some of the maceration liquid before mixing. Whip cream to soft peaks only.

items.symptom: Meringue disintegrated to powder when crushed
items.cause: Over-baked until completely dry all the way through
items.fix: Pull the meringue from the oven while it still gives very slightly when pressed on the underside. A fully dry meringue has no texture when broken.

items.cause: Cream is too warm, or the bowl is warm
items.fix: Chill the bowl and cream before whipping, especially in warm weather. Double cream whips more reliably cold.

content.text: Eton Mess takes its name from Eton College in Berkshire, where it has been associated with the Fourth of June celebration and the annual cricket match against Harrow since at least the early twentieth century. The story that it was the result of a crushed meringue dessert is plausible but unverified. The dish remained a school and summer-party dessert for most of the twentieth century before becoming widely fashionable in British restaurants and homes from the 1980s onward, when strawberry season desserts had a broader cultural moment.
